segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a black screen, accompanied by ethereal, atmospheric music featuring sustained synth pads and subtle chimes. A stylized white logo, resembling a hand or claw, appears against a starry background, followed by a sweeping view of a spiral galaxy. The camera dynamically moves through dense star fields and cosmic dust clouds, zooming towards a bright star. The scene transitions to Earth viewed from space, its dark side illuminated by city lights with the Sun visible in the distance. Text appears on screen stating 'Earth Orbital Velocity: 30 km/s (18 miles per second)'. A male voice begins to narrate, asking "What are you doing right now?" and suggesting that if watching, one is likely "sitting down somewhere" and feels "stationary, right?" He then emphatically states "Wrong," clarifying that while one may be stationary on Earth's surface, "Earth itself is moving, hurtling through space at speeds of around 30 kilometers per second," directly correlating with the on-screen text. The camera pulls back to show the Sun more prominently, with text updating to 'Solar System Speed: 220 km/s (136 miles per second)'. Briefly, the video cuts to a man sitting on a bench, wearing a beige cap, light blue shirt, and beige pants, with a backpack and a black bag on his lap, before returning to the space visuals of Earth with the Sun behind it. The narrator continues, stating "Similarly, the Sun is also moving, at speeds of..." as the audio fades out, reinforcing the vastness and constant motion of celestial bodies.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the bright, radiant Sun, complete with lens flares, positioned behind a partially illuminated Earth casting a shadow, as a green glowing streak moves diagonally across the frame. A male narrator immediately begins, explaining that Earth orbits the Sun at 220 kilometers per second, dragging our entire solar system with it as it orbits the galactic tide of the Milky Way. The scene transitions to a wider view of a ringed planet, resembling Saturn, set against a dense, dusty galactic plane, while the narrator clarifies that we don't feel these movements due to gravity, but "elsewhere in space," everything moves and flows, with nothing remaining stationary. The camera rapidly shifts through swirling clouds of dust and stars within the galactic plane, then expands into deep space to reveal a spiral galaxy with distinct arms and a bright core, surrounded by countless stars. The narrator questions what makes the galaxy move, explaining that everything in our local universe, including the Milky Way, is being dragged through space for millions of light-years. As the camera continues to zoom out through vast, star-filled expanses, clusters, and nebulous formations, the narrator describes this movement as being pulled through the void towards an unseen gravitational focal point, exerting an astonishing, almost godlike influence. The segment concludes with a dense field of stars, suggesting a cosmic journey, all set against a deep, ambient, and slightly mysterious background soundscape.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a star-filled expanse of space, where the text 'THE GREAT ATTRACTOR' fades in and out, accompanied by a calm male voice introducing "the mystery of the Great Attractor" over atmospheric, deep synth music. The scene transitions to a distant spiral galaxy, labeled 'Galaxy,' which slowly rotates and zooms in, revealing its swirling arms and bright core. The male narrator explains that the Milky Way is a "vast, ancient, and structured collection of hundreds of billions of stars, known as a galaxy," adding that the universe is "teeming with them," many larger and more densely packed than our own. The camera then moves dynamically through the galaxy's stellar arms, as the narrator describes how gravity allows these many stars to work as a "coherent structure" with a stronger gravitational influence. The view shifts to a different galaxy, a large elliptical one, interacting with a smaller spiral galaxy, visually demonstrating the narrator's point that "galaxies are so incredibly massive that they can attract one another from millions of light-years across space, sometimes getting close enough to collide, producing new, larger galaxies." The two galaxies merge into a single, luminous, amorphous mass, reflecting this collision. The final frames show the resulting cloud-like structure, glowing with diffuse light against the dark backdrop of space, emphasizing the vastness and dynamic nature of the universe, as the narrator concludes by mentioning the voluminous space between galaxies and "another force at work." The entire segment is underscored by consistent atmospheric background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide view of a dark, star-filled cosmos, featuring a bright, golden-hued spiral galaxy on the left and a diffuse, cloud-like nebula on the right, all set against a continuous, low, ambient, and ethereal hum. A male narrator explains that space itself is expanding, including the space between galaxies, which pushes them apart faster than they can attract one another, leading to a sparser universe. The camera then zooms in on the golden galaxy, which resolves into a detailed spiral galaxy with swirling arms and a luminous core. The text 'Hubble Flow' appears, coinciding with the narrator's statement, 'This is known as the Hubble Flow.' He continues, explaining that galaxies generally recede uniformly as the universe grows, and most are not close enough to collide, except for very small, localized dense pockets of space that will eventually collapse into a single structure. The scene transitions to a dense field of stars, simulating a deep-space view, then shifts to a pinkish nebula with scattered stars, before panning across various galaxies, including a spiral galaxy with a bright center and a smaller companion, followed by a larger elliptical galaxy interacting with a spiral galaxy. The narrator further clarifies that while not always destined to collide, dense concentrations of galaxies can still exert influences, bringing them close enough that their combined gravitational potential attracts more remote galaxies, creating denser concentrations and emptying out the space in between through a self-sustaining process. Visually, these interacting galaxies merge into a cloud-like structure, illustrating this galactic collision or interaction. The final frames showcase a dense cluster of stars with dynamic, swirling motion, emphasizing the universe's vastness and complexity, as the narrator concludes that this forms the 'overarching structure that stretches...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a vibrant, animated depiction of the cosmic web, featuring glowing blue filaments and bright orange nodes against a dark background, with the text 'COSMIC WEB' prominently displayed. A male voice introduces "the cosmic web" as the structure spanning our entire universe. The scene then transitions to a monochromatic, high-contrast visualization of this intricate web of white filaments and clusters against black space. As the camera moves through this cosmic structure, accompanied by ethereal background music, the male voice describes it as "kind of like a sponge," with areas of matter branching in every direction and large empty swaths in between. The male voice further explains that "the cosmic web is composed of enormous walls of interacting galaxies, known as galaxy filaments," as the text 'Galaxy Filaments' appears, highlighting these long, thread-like formations connecting clusters of galaxies. The narrator adds that these filaments "can contain hundreds of thousands of galaxies and span over billions of light-years." The view then zooms in on dense regions, revealing individual galaxies as bright points, while the male voice elaborates that "the galaxies within these filaments migrate through paths within the structure," with "streams of interacting galaxies flow, converge, and diverge like great cosmic water flows over distances difficult to comprehend." As the text 'Galaxy Clusters & Galaxy Superclusters' appears, denoting the larger structures, the male voice identifies "the individual thread-like components of a galaxy filament" as "galaxy clusters and superclusters." The sequence includes close-ups of elliptical and spiral galaxies, some interacting, with one frame showing a large, luminous galaxy dominating the left side, possibly indicating a supercluster core. The male voice further defines these as "smaller groupings of tens, hundreds, or perhaps thousands of galaxies, which are more concentrated than the overall density of the filament, and thus have more substantial interactions with one another."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a vast, star-filled expanse of deep space, accompanied by ambient, spacey, and slightly melancholic background music. A male narrator begins by explaining that the lines between "clusters, superclusters, and filaments are slightly fuzzy," primarily because the existence of "galaxy structures of filament size" was not realized when these terms were coined. The scene transitions to a diagram centered on the 'Local Group,' showing labeled arrows pointing to nearby galaxy groups like the Virgo Cluster and M81 Group with their distances. The narrator continues, expressing past disbelief that "galaxies could cluster up" and "interact over billions of light-years," admitting, "How wrong we were." This leads into a dynamic visualization of the cosmic web, depicting galaxies as bright nodes connected by luminous filaments forming a butterfly-like structure, illustrating the large-scale structures the narrator describes. The view then zooms into a rotating Earth, half-illuminated with city lights, set against a dusty galactic plane, as the narrator reflects on how, "Over the past 40 years, we have managed to build up a comprehensive three-dimensional map of intergalactic space," and now know "a considerable amount about our large-scale surroundings," calling Earth "a tiny world existing in the blink of an eye on a galactic timescale." A detailed 2D diagram of the Local Group appears, labeling galaxies like the Milky Way, Andromeda, and various dwarf galaxies within an elliptical coordinate system. The narrator elaborates that "our local group, which contains us, Andromeda, the Triangulum galaxy, and more than 100 other small satellite dwarf galaxies," is situated in an "outlying region" to a cluster of "more than 1,300 galaxies." This is followed by a dense, glowing visualization of the 'Virgo Cluster' in warm hues, featuring a mix of spiral and elliptical galaxies, which the narrator explicitly names as the "Virgo Cluster" and identifies as the "gravitational core." Finally, a larger-scale diagram shows the Local Group and surrounding galaxy groups within a 10 million light-year radius, reinforcing the hierarchical organization of the universe.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a vast, star-filled expanse of space, showcasing a dense field of white and faintly colored stars against a black background, suggesting a deep-space view. The scene transitions to a diagram centered on the 'Local Group,' with labeled arrows pointing to nearby galaxy groups such as the Virgo Cluster, M81 Group, and Leo I, each annotated with distances in light-years. This diagram fades into a dynamic visualization of the cosmic web, where galaxies appear as bright nodes connected by luminous filaments, forming a butterfly-like structure. The view then zooms into a rotating Earth, half-illuminated with city lights, set against a backdrop of a dusty galactic plane. Following this, a detailed 2D diagram of the Local Group is shown, with labeled galaxies like the Milky Way, Andromeda, and various dwarf galaxies, arranged in an elliptical coordinate system with a scale of 1 million light-years. Finally, a larger-scale diagram appears, showing the Local Group and surrounding galaxy groups within a 10 million light-year radius, with labels for clusters like the Fornax Cluster and Eridanus Cluster, and a scale bar indicating 10 million light-years. The overall narrative progresses from a broad cosmic view to increasingly detailed astronomical structures, emphasizing the hierarchical organization of galaxies and clusters in the universe.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed 2D diagram of the Local Group and surrounding galaxy clusters, including the Virgo Supercluster and Milky Way, marked with concentric circles indicating distances in millions of light-years. A male narrator explains that our Local Group is part of a much larger 'colony,' the Virgo Supercluster, containing over 100 galaxy groups like ours, and is one of an estimated 10 million galaxy superclusters in the observable universe. The view then dramatically zooms out, transitioning to a 3D representation of the universe's large-scale structure, revealing a dense, speckled field of galaxies forming intricate filaments and voids. The narrator details the Virgo Supercluster's immense size, extending over 110 million light-years and being over 7,000 times as massive as the Local Group, exerting considerable influence on its intergalactic neighborhood. The camera moves through this vast cosmic web, highlighting the intricate network of galaxy clusters and superclusters. A scientific paper titled 'The Laniakea supercluster of galaxies' then appears on screen, accompanied by an image of the University of Hawaii, as the narrator reveals a groundbreaking 2014 study by scientists at the University of Hawaii showed the Virgo Supercluster is just one component of an even greater network of almost unknowable intricacy and scale. This transitions to a dynamic visualization of the Laniakea supercluster, depicted as a glowing, golden web of filaments with the word 'Laniakea' appearing in bold white text, which the narrator identifies as 'Our home galactic supercluster.' The video concludes with a breakdown of the name 'Laniakea' into 'Lani' (Heaven) and 'akea' (Immeasurable, Spacious), emphasizing its Hawaiian meaning. Throughout the segment, a calm, expansive instrumental background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a black screen displaying the white text 'Lani' (Heaven) and 'akea' (Immeasurable, Spacious), introducing the Laniakea Supercluster, accompanied by gentle, ambient space-like music. A male narrator begins, explaining that "The Laniakea Supercluster extends over half a billion light-years across space and is home to more than 100,000 galaxies," as the scene transitions to a colorful 2D velocity map of the Laniakea Supercluster, outlined in orange and labeled with regions like Shapley, Coma, and Perseus-Pisces, along with its size (160 Mpc) and mass (10^17 M☉). The view then shifts to a 3D wireframe cube illustrating 'Wiener Filter Large Scale velocity flow lines,' with axes marked in km/s, visually representing the cosmic flows. The narrator continues, "It is so vast that for a long time we didn't realize that its constituent superclusters were connected." This is followed by a cylindrical 2D map of the universe, densely populated with labeled galaxy groups and superclusters such as Virgo, Centaurus, and Hydra, with a yellow dashed line tracing the Laniakea Supercluster boundary. The narrator elaborates, "Laniakea consists of four main galaxy superclusters: us and the rest of the Virgo Supercluster, the Hydra-Centaurus Supercluster, the Pavo-Indus Supercluster, and the Southern Supercluster." The video then transitions to a dynamic 3D visualization of the Laniakea Supercluster as a glowing golden web of filaments, with the Milky Way marked, emphasizing its vast, interconnected structure. The narrator adds, "And despite their enormous cosmic distances from each other, they interlink to create a plethora of flows across space." He concludes by stating, "Because the Laniakea Supercluster is so large, it is not dense enough to be gravitationally bound. And so, as the universe expands, instead of coming together and condensing further, it should instead get caught...", as the final frames show a sparse, star-filled black background, suggesting a zoom-out into the deeper cosmos, before fading to black, with the ambient music continuing.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a backdrop of deep, atmospheric, and slightly mysterious music, the video opens with a dark, star-filled expanse, showing a spiral galaxy forming and evolving into a dense, luminous elliptical galaxy. A male voice explains that while the universe's expansion elongates structures, the 'entirety of the supercluster appears to be being attracted to whatever is in this area,' rather than dispersing. The scene transitions to a colorful 2D velocity map, 'The Laniakea Watershed,' displaying galaxies as white dots with flow lines, and a black arrow pointing to a bright orange 'Great Attractor' region. The speaker questions, 'What could possibly be there that is powerful enough to unite galactic superstructures of this size?' The map, showing a red-to-blue velocity gradient and labeled structures like the Virgo and Centaurus Clusters, visually supports the idea of these large-scale flows. The view then shifts to a 3D representation of the Laniakea Supercluster, a vast, translucent blue structure with labeled regions like Hercules and Shapley, showing intricate flow lines converging toward massive attractors. The voice clarifies that while 'flows of galaxies aren't uncommon within galaxy webs,' this is 'more than just a flow,' identifying a 'gravitational anomaly here with enormous power, which is anchoring the entire cluster together and making it move, like a sinkhole to a half a billion light-year basin.' The video returns to a 2D map emphasizing the gravitational pull toward 'The Great Attractor,' which the speaker explicitly names as 'this anomaly,' before the final frames fade to black, concluding the cosmic journey through large-scale structure and galactic motion.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a black screen, then transitions to a vibrant 2D velocity map of the Laniakea Supercluster, outlined in orange with white flow lines converging toward a central region, indicating gravitational attraction. A male voice, accompanied by subtle, atmospheric background music, introduces "The Great Attractor" as the "somewhat ominous name given to the gravitational focal point of the Laniakea Supercluster," estimated to be "somewhere around 250 million light-years away from the Milky Way." The map displays a color gradient from blue to green to red, representing velocity, with labeled regions like the Virgo Void and the Great Attractor. The speaker explains that this entity is "attracting the contents of the cluster towards it," with "everything for hundreds of millions of light-years" flowing towards this single region. The scene shifts to a luminous, dusty spiral galaxy, then to a split-screen view showing a detailed 2D velocity map on the left and a starfield on the right, featuring labeled galaxy clusters like the Virgo Cluster and Centaurus Cluster, with flow lines radiating from the Great Attractor. The audio speculates on the Great Attractor's nature, suggesting it could be an "unusually high concentration of mass," like an "ultra-massive or dense galaxy cluster," or "something stranger," such as a "monstrous intergalactic black hole" or a "mysterious dark force" counteracting the universe's expansion. The video then transitions to a dense starfield, zooming out to reveal a vast cosmic web of galaxies. The voice concludes that it is "likely to be the product of a dense galaxy supercluster," so massive that it is "influencing the entirety of the Virgo Supercluster" and other surrounding clusters. A dramatic view of a ringed planet, resembling Saturn, appears above a galactic plane, with a distant star visible. The scene fades into a dark nebula, then transitions to a circular view of a dense galaxy cluster, which gradually zooms out to reveal a spherical distribution of galaxies, simulating a 3D cosmic structure. The final frames show a dark, star-filled sphere, suggesting the universe's vastness and structure.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black screen, transitioning to a breathtaking view of Earth from space, its curvature, swirling clouds, and oceans set against the Milky Way’s galactic plane, accompanied by a consistent, ambient, and slightly mysterious orchestral score. A male voice begins, explaining a "fundamental issue" – that "we can't see it." As the camera slowly pans and zooms out, revealing Earth as a small, illuminated sphere with the Sun visible in the distance, the narrator elaborates that the "Great Attractor mystery" is intriguing yet infuriating due to its location. The perspective shifts to a top-down view of the Milky Way, displaying its spiral arms labeled 'New Arm,' 'Scutum-Centaurus,' and 'Perseus,' with the Sun marked as a small yellow dot. The narrator clarifies that the mystery "lies in an an area of the sky which is blocked out by the Milky Way's galactic plane from the point of view of the Earth." The view transitions to a wide, panoramic shot of the galactic plane, emphasizing its dense, dusty band stretching across the frame. The male voice explains that because of the solar system's position relative to the "Milky Way's galactic plane," its "bright illuminated core and dark dusty clouds extinguish the light," blocking approximately one-fifth of our field of view. The text 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' appears prominently over the galactic plane, which the narrator identifies as "this area." The camera continues to zoom out, revealing the full spiral structure of the Milky Way with its bright core and swirling arms, then transitions to a broader view of the galaxy as part of a larger cosmic structure with faint neighboring galaxies. The narrator concludes that the galaxy's features make it "virtually impossible to see or detect anything within or beyond it," clarifying that "there's nothing different about the space behind the zone of avoidance, it is just our position relative to the galactic core." The final frames show the galaxy rotating slowly, with the galactic plane dominating the view, as the ambient music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a luminous spiral galaxy, its bright core and swirling arms glowing against a star-dotted space, as a calm male voice introduces "the problem." The camera pulls back to a wider starfield view, while the speaker explains that in about a hundred million years, the Sun will have orbited the galaxy's central black hole, taking "us" to the other side of the Milky Way. The scene transitions to Earth from space, initially a dark sphere with illuminated city lights and the Sun visible in the distance, with the speaker noting this journey will take a "very, very long time," leaving "us" stuck on this side. As Earth rotates, revealing its blue oceans and white cloud cover with the galactic plane stretching across the background, the voice describes how the backdrop of the 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' will be revealed. The perspective shifts to a broader view of the galactic plane, a dense, dusty band of stars and interstellar material, with the text 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' overlaid. The speaker reveals that the largest gravitational anomaly in the local universe, dragging "us" and everything around, lies behind this obstructed region, calling it an "ironic" and "unnerving mystery" that troubled scientists for 40 years. The camera zooms out, showing Earth as a small, rotating planet against the vastness of the Milky Way, before transitioning to a side view of the galaxy as a thin, luminous disk. The speaker concludes by mentioning the "Great Attractor" was detected in the 1970s when the Milky Way galaxy's movement through space was realized, and that back then, nothing behind the 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' could be seen or detected. The final frames focus on the galactic plane, emphasizing its structure and the 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' text, before fading to black, all set to a subtle, atmospheric soundscape.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a serene night-time view of an observatory, its domed structures silhouetted against a star-filled sky, as a male narrator explains that past generations lacked the sophisticated technology available today. This historical limitation meant that "around 40 degrees of the sky remained unmapped and unknowable," a concept visually reinforced by a dense starfield displaying the text '< ZOA >' and later by the galactic plane, a dusty band stretching across the frame with 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' overlaid. The scene transitions to a vibrant, colorful map of the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB), which the narrator identifies as "the primordial microwave radiation left over from the Big Bang which permeates all of space." This CMB map then shifts to a simplified, rainbow-colored CMB Dipole, described by the narrator as "a gradient in the temperature of the sky from one side to the other." A red arrow appears on the CMB Dipole, indicating the direction of motion, as the narrator clarifies that this "disparity is the result of the Milky Way moving through space," causing the "red area" to appear "slightly warmer" due to compressed radiation. The video concludes with a deep-space view of a bright, distant galaxy, followed by a vast field of spiral and elliptical galaxies, while the narrator notes that "galaxies moving through space is not that unusual, as all galaxies are moving apart, largely in accordance with the Hubble flow," all set against a continuous, ethereal background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of two spiral galaxies dramatically colliding, their bright cores and swirling arms merging against a dark cosmic backdrop, while a male narrator begins to explain a 'flow, namely gravity, and the gravity of objects around a galaxy.' The scene then transitions to a single, luminous elliptical galaxy, with the text 'Peculiar Velocity' appearing on screen as the narrator defines it as 'the discrepancy between a galaxy's actual movement through space due to gravity, when compared with what one would expect in an expanding universe,' or simply 'the speed at which a galaxy moves through space because of something other than the universe's expansion.' The view shifts to a starfield, where Earth appears as a small, dark sphere with illuminated city lights, positioned against a backdrop of constellations outlined in white and blue lines, including Centaurus, Lupus, and Crux. Text overlays indicate Earth's motion at '600 km/s (372 miles per second)' as the narrator reveals, 'we discovered that we are moving towards the constellation of Centaurus at a speed of about 600 kilometers per second.' The camera then zooms out, revealing the Milky Way's galactic plane as a dense, dusty band stretching across the frame, labeled 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE,' which the narrator explains is where 'wherever we were headed, it was situated behind the zone of avoidance.' The final frames show the galactic plane from a side perspective, emphasizing its thin, luminous structure before fading to black, with the narrator concluding that the cause of this peculiar velocity remained a topic of fierce debate. A subtle, atmospheric background soundscape accompanies the narration throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dark, star-filled expanse of space, gradually revealing a dense field of stars and distant galaxies, all set to a low, ambient background music. A male speaker begins to explain that there were "just simply more galaxies in the direction of the zone of avoidance, which we just couldn't see." As a prominent spiral galaxy emerges with its bright core and swirling arms, the speaker discusses "The Milky Way's outlying position to the much denser Virgo Cluster" as a possible cause for observed cosmic movements. The scene then transitions to a detailed 2D map labeled '2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey,' displaying a cosmic web of galaxies arranged in glowing filaments and voids, with axes marked in redshift and billion light-years. The speaker continues, noting that while "the Virgo Cluster could attract us with its gravity, it is not nearly massive enough" to explain the speed at which we are moving, suggesting "something considerably more massive was to blame." He adds that "the mystery got even bigger, quite literally," as "redshift surveys began mapping the universe in 3D during the 1980s." This led to the realization that not only are "we moving towards this gravitational anomaly in Centaurus," but so are "the galaxies both above and below the zone of avoidance," indicating "the power of the source was much greater than once thought." The view then returns to the starfield, zooming in on another spiral galaxy, accompanied by on-screen text indicating a distance range of '150,000,000 – 220,000,000 ly,' which the speaker's final words confirm as the estimated distance for the anomaly's source, lying "between 150 million and 220 million."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black screen, then transitions to a black-and-white portrait of Prof. Donald Lynden-Bell (1935–2018), seated with an open book, as a male narrator explains that "two years later" an international team led by Lynden-Bell proposed the concept of the "Great Attractor." This is immediately followed by a full-screen display of a scientific paper co-authored by D. Lynden-Bell, titled 'Spectroscopy and Photometry of Elliptical Galaxies V: Galaxy Streaming Toward the New Supergalactic Center,' with the phrase 'a great attractor' highlighted in yellow. The narrator details how this proposal suggested that the observed motions of 400 elliptical galaxies were best fitted by a large bulk flow towards this "Great Attractor," which was "lurking behind the zone of avoidance," thus coining the term. The scene then shifts to a starfield with faint galaxies, zooming into a bright elliptical galaxy, while the narrator continues to discuss the mystery of the Great Attractor. A radiant, lens-flared object labeled '10 Quintillion Solar Masses' and its numerical equivalent then appears, perfectly synchronizing with the narrator's explanation that the Great Attractor was predicted to be "around 10 quintillion times the mass of the Sun," or "10 million billion suns," equivalent to "many thousands of massive galaxies." The view expands to a dense starfield before transitioning to a cosmic scene featuring Earth rotating in the foreground with visible city lights, the galactic plane visible, and a bright Sun in the distance casting light on Earth’s edge. A translucent sphere containing a glowing network of filaments, representing the cosmic web, appears near the Sun, visually depicting the vast, massive structure being described. Throughout the segment, a low, ambient hum with subtle electronic undertones provides a mysterious background sound.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white close-up of a large radio telescope, its intricate mesh dish silhouetted against a cloudy sky, as a male voice explains that "times have changed and technology has vastly improved," allowing us to move beyond simply observing visible light. The scene transitions to a breathtaking view of Earth from space, with city lights glowing and the Sun rising, before moving into deep space to reveal the Milky Way as a luminous band of stars and dust, first in vibrant blues and greens, then fiery reds. The narrator clarifies that while "the Milky Way's plane might be opaque to light that we can see," radio and infrared emissions from distant galaxy superclusters are "capable of penetrating the galaxy's clouds." An animated diagram illustrates this, showing Earth within the Milky Way with red wavy 'RADIO WAVES' traveling toward 'HIDDEN GALAXIES' beyond the galactic plane, which the voice identifies as the "avoidance zone," noting that radio gives "some idea of what could lie beyond." The scene then shifts to a green-tinted X-ray view, labeled 'X-RAYS', as the narrator states that "the best means we have of seeing behind the zone of avoidance is through X-rays," defining them as "another kind of electromagnetic radiation" that, like radio, "can also pass through the opaque galactic plane" and are "emitted by galaxy superclusters." A white X-ray-like image of the galactic plane appears, followed by a dark, dusty view with the 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' text overlaid. The video concludes by revealing hidden galaxies as glowing blue and red nebulae emerging from behind the dust, emphasizing that there are "far fewer X-ray emitting sources in space than there are sources of infrared and radio waves, making supercluster..."

An animated diagram appears, showing Earth within the Milky Way, with red wavy lines labeled 'RADIO WAVES' traveling toward 'HIDDEN GALAXIES' beyond the galactic plane, illustrating how radio astronomy penetrates dust. The scene shifts to a green-tinted X-ray view, labeled 'X-RAYS', revealing obscured structures, followed by a white X-ray-like image of the galactic plane, then a dark, dusty view with the 'ZONE OF AVOIDANCE' text overlaid. Finally, the video reveals hidden galaxies as glowing blue and red nebulae emerging from behind the dust, showcasing the power of multi-wavelength astronomy to uncover the universe’s hidden depths.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a vibrant cosmic scene of glowing blue and red nebulae against a star-filled black background, accompanied by a continuous, subtle atmospheric hum, as a narrator discusses the ease of identifying X-ray emitters. This transitions to detailed views of the Röntgensatellit (ROSAT) satellite, its solar panels and instruments clearly visible, as the narrator explains that in 1996, data from the "ROSAT microwave mission" was used by a team of researchers. A portrait of Renée Kraan-Korteweg, a woman with short gray hair, then appears alongside the satellite, identified by the narrator as "South African astrophysicist Renée Kraan-Korteweg," who led the team. The visuals shift to a dynamic starfield visualization, where stars and galaxies move and cluster into a dense, colorful cosmic web, representing the "galaxy supercluster within the zone of avoidance" that her team discovered, which had previously evaded detection. This supercluster is now considered a "key galactic concentration at the heart of the Great Attractor," a region that was "undetectable through visible light" due to the "zone of avoidance." A green-tinted, pixelated map then highlights a central cluster, labeled 'Abell 3627' and subsequently 'Norma Cluster', as the narrator details how plotting X-ray sources identified by ROSAT revealed this "significant galaxy cluster." The narrator further explains that "Abell 3627," also known as "the Norma Cluster," lies "around 220 million light-years away from the Earth" and is thought to be "very close to the proposed heart of the Great Attractor." A large 2D map of the universe then appears, showing the Milky Way at the center with numerous labeled galaxy clusters and superclusters, including the 'Norma & Great Attractor' region outlined in yellow. The segment concludes with a massive elliptical galaxy featuring a bright core, accompanied by the text '1,000,000,000,000,000 Solar Masses', as the narrator begins to mention its immense mass.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a massive elliptical galaxy, accompanied by the text '1,000,000,000,000,000 Solar Masses', as a male narrator describes a "region of one quadrillion times that of our sun." A consistent, low ambient hum underscores the scene. The camera rapidly zooms out through a starfield, revealing the galaxy as part of a larger cosmic structure, while the narrator explains that "the Great Attractor was probably a supercluster with Abell 3627 near its core," adding that "the Norma cluster probably didn't account for the full picture." As the view expands, the initial galaxy becomes a small point among countless others, illustrating the universe's vastness. The scene shifts to Earth from space, with the Milky Way's dusty galactic plane visible in the background, as the narrator notes that even a massive entity "didn't seem massive enough to account for the peculiar velocity of the Milky Way." The camera continues to pull back, revealing a dense starfield that resolves into a complex network of galaxy filaments and clusters. The narrator continues, stating that "something else must be at work," but observation was "limited in what we could observe due to the obscured zone," leaving the "mystery inconclusive heading into the new millennium." The final frames display a scientific 2D velocity map of the local universe, showing flow lines converging toward massive attractors like the Shapley and Coma clusters, with labeled regions such as the Local Void, Perseus-Pisces, and Virgo-Coma, and axes labeled in km/s. Over this, the narrator explains that "many scientists agree that the Great Attractor is the result of a dense galaxy concentration," but also mentions that "it is by no means the only theory," referencing those who "refuted the idea of a galaxy supercluster being responsible due to the incomparable scale."

The scene then transitions to a starfield with a grid overlay, where the text 'Dark Energy' appears as the narrator defines it as the phenomenon powering and accelerating the universe's expansion, describing it as 'unknown and beyond our realm of understanding.' The final frames return to a starfield, with the camera zooming toward a bright, central point, while the narrator defines 'Dark Flow' as a non-random component of the universe's expansion that deviates from the Hubble flow, causing galaxies to stream towards a single point like the Great Attractor.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a starfield simulation, showing a dense, dynamic distribution of white points representing galaxies or stars, as the camera travels through the cosmos. A narrator begins, explaining how objects might recede away from one another, setting up a mystery. This transitions to a colorful 2D velocity map of the local universe, displaying labeled structures like the 'Local Void', 'Virgo Cluster', 'Fornax Cluster', and 'NGC 5846/5848 Cluster', with white flow lines indicating motion and a color gradient from blue to red representing velocity, with axes labeled in km/s. The narrator suggests that if there's "something darker at play," we might find evidence of a "gigantic flow mechanism" by searching for something at the "opposite end of the Great Attractor," which he calls "a great repeller." The scene fades to black, and the text 'DIPOLE REPELLER' appears in white on a starfield, as the narrator reveals that in 2017, "we did see something just like this: The Dipole Repeller." A larger-scale 2D velocity map then appears, showing labels like 'Shapley', 'Coma', and 'Perseus-Pisces', with flow lines converging toward massive attractors and diverging from a repeller region. A 3D schematic diagram follows, illustrating the 'Dipole Repeller' as a large void with blue flow lines radiating outward, while the 'Great Attractor' pulls matter inward, with the 'Local Group' shown moving at 631 km/s. The narrator explains that just as galaxies in the Laniakea Supercluster flow towards the Great Attractor, they also flow away from an area on the opposite end, a region "pushing its galaxies outwards." The video returns to a starfield before transitioning to a final 2D map, a cosmic web visualization showing the 'Great Attractor' near the Centaurus Cluster, with flow lines converging toward it and the Milky Way positioned within the broader cosmic web, including labels for 'Hydra', 'Antlia', and 'Bulk flow toward Antlia-Centaurus', using a color gradient for velocity and white spheres for galaxy clusters. The narrator concludes by pondering if the Great Attractor and Dipole Repeller are part of a "dark cosmic system capable of overcoming the effects of the universe's expansion."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic visualization of the cosmic web, displaying a dense field of white dots representing galaxies against a black background, simulating vast, three-dimensional space. The camera moves through this field, revealing clusters and filaments with rapid zooming and panning, emphasizing the universe's scale and structure. As the density of points varies, cosmic voids are highlighted before transitioning to a more structured view, where a large, luminous elliptical galaxy glows softly. Throughout this, a male narrator begins to explain the 'Dipole Repeller', noting it "may seem anomalous and indicative of something more spooky," but that "dark flow certainly doesn't need to exist to explain it." The scene then shifts to a colorful 2D map illustrating the large-scale structure of the universe, with labeled regions like the 'Local Void' and 'Virgo Cluster', overlaid with flow lines indicating gravitational motion. The narrator clarifies that the 'Dipole Repeller' "probably isn't even an anomaly, but rather a natural byproduct of galaxy cluster formation." This is followed by a 3D visualization of the 'Laniakea Watershed', a translucent blue structure with labeled superclusters and flow lines converging toward massive attractors. The speaker elaborates on how "galaxies gathering and attracting more galaxies creates denser galaxy clusters," which in turn "empties out the space around the cluster, creating voids between clumps of galaxies." He adds that "the more populous and massive the galaxy cluster, the more voids will form around it." The final frame introduces the term 'Virtual Repeller' in bold white text, as the narrator concludes that while it "may seem like galaxies are being pushed away from the Dipole Repeller, in reality nothing is pushing them, only the pulling force of gravity," and that the 'Dipole Repeller' is simply "an area which has had its galaxies emptied out by massive influences." A consistent, deep, and ambient background music underscores the scientific and exploratory atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a 3D translucent blue visualization of the Laniakea Watershed, illustrating the large-scale flow of galaxies towards massive attractors like Shapley, Coma, and Perseus-Pisces, with a bold 'Virtual Repeller' labeled prominently, which a male narrator describes as a "so-called virtual repeller." The narrator then questions the forces driving these flows, asking what causes movement "if not the clusters in the region of the greater attractor." This transitions into a dramatic view of the Milky Way’s galactic plane, rendered in deep reds and browns, emphasizing dense dust lanes. The scene darkens to a starfield before displaying the title 'Clusters in the Zone of Avoidance [CIZA]', which the narrator identifies as "a study called the Clusters in the Zone of Avoidance project, or CIZA." A colorful all-sky map follows, showing the galactic plane in red and green hues with black lines indicating obscured regions, then a split-screen reveals a galaxy cluster in X-ray (left) and optical (right) views with contour lines, as the narrator explains that the CIZA project "selected X-ray signatures recorded by the Rosat mission for follow-up and investigation with newer, more effective X-ray technology." The video cuts to a night sky with a distant cityscape, then overlays constellation lines across the Milky Way, illustrating how constellations are defined. The galactic plane reappears, now with a scientific map overlaid, plotting galaxy clusters (blue dots) against galactic longitude and latitude, with a green curve marking the Zone of Avoidance. The narrator states the project's aim was "to map this final portion of the sky in the zone of avoidance that remained uncharted for so many years, and also to focus in on what makes the region of the greater attractor so great." The final frames return to a dynamic, dusty view of the galactic plane, reinforcing the theme of obscured cosmic structures, as the narrator concludes that the project "identified over 200 previously undiscovered galaxy clusters in galactic no man's land, including in the proposed region of the greater attractor."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a deep-space view of a starfield, densely populated with white stars, as the camera slowly zooms out to reveal a spiral galaxy forming at the center, all set to subtle, atmospheric background music. This transitions to a blue-tinted map of large-scale cosmic structures, highlighting the Milky Way with a red cross, the Great Attractor, and the Shapley Supercluster, complete with labeled arrows indicating gravitational influences and a scale bar in million light-years. A calm male narrator explains that initial 'clusters' were found to be 'significantly less massive' than predicted for 'our peculiar velocity,' only 'a tenth of the proposed mass.' He clarifies that the 'Great Attractor is not nearly as great as we once thought,' though it is the 'focal point of the Laniakea Supercluster' which is dragging 'us' (the Milky Way) towards it, it is not the only thing influencing us. The narrator then reveals that the Caesar survey found a 'substantial concentration of galaxies much further beyond the Great Attractor, near the Shapley Supercluster.' The visual shifts to a detailed star chart labeled 'Shapley Supercluster,' displaying numerous galaxy clusters marked with identifiers like A3571, A3562, and SC1329-312, overlaid on a grid of galactic coordinates, as the camera pans across it. The narrator describes the 'Shapley Supercluster' as 'one of the most studied of its kind,' with its '25 constituent galaxy clusters marked here in blue.' He adds that it is 'situated around 650 million light-years away from the Milky Way' and was 'discovered in the 1930s by...' (the audio cuts off before naming the discoverer). The segment concludes with a black-and-white portrait of a young man in a suit and tie, gazing directly at the viewer, likely the unnamed historical astronomer associated with the Shapley Supercluster's discovery, as the atmospheric music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white portrait of a young man in a suit and tie, identified by a male narrator as historical astronomer Harlow Shapley, set against a starry background. A low, ambient hum underscores the narration as the scene transitions to a vibrant, deep-space image of a dense starfield. The narrator explains that the topic of interest only emerged in the late 90s with better technology. This is followed by a montage of scientific papers and research abstracts, including 'CIZA: The First Systematic X-Ray Search for Clusters of Galaxies Behind the Milky Way,' illustrating the narrator's point that 40 scientific papers were published on the cluster between 1997 and 2001. The narrator adds that in 2005, CIZA discovered a concentrated region of galaxies near the Shapley Supercluster, nicknamed the Shapley Attractor. The narrative then shifts to a 3D schematic diagram illustrating the large-scale structure of the universe, with labeled regions like the 'Shapley Attractor,' 'Great Attractor,' and 'Dipole Repeller,' showing gravitational flows. The narrator describes the Shapley Attractor as an even greater, more massive galactic overdensity than the Great Attractor, lying about half a billion light-years away. The video continues with a colorful, heat-map visualization of the Shapley Concentration, labeled with specific galaxy clusters, and a detailed star chart mapping the Shapley Supercluster with numerous galaxy clusters plotted against galactic coordinates. The narrator explains that Shapley is home to over 8,000 galaxies, with a mass 10,000 times that of the Milky Way, and four times greater than the Great Attractor. The sequence progresses to a 2D diagram of galaxy types, followed by a dynamic, star-filled animation of a spiral galaxy forming and rotating in the void, symbolizing cosmic evolution. The narrator concludes that this supercluster is believed to be contributing the lion's share of influence responsible for the Milky Way's peculiar velocity. The final frames depict a large, luminous spiral galaxy with a bright core and faint spiral arms, surrounded by smaller galaxies against countless stars, emphasizing the universe's grandeur.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a serene view of a spiral galaxy, likely the Milky Way, seen edge-on against a starfield, emphasizing its dusty galactic plane, while a male narrator explains that everything in "our supercluster" flows towards the Great Attractor, which is itself pulled towards the Shapley Supercluster. A subtle, ambient synth drone plays throughout. The camera then zooms out through a dense starfield, as the narrator clarifies that the Milky Way's quick pull towards Centaurus is a compound effect, with 44% of its peculiar velocity due to the closer Great Attractor and 56% to the much more massive and remote Shapley Supercluster. This leads into a 3D scientific visualization labeled 'Wiener Filter Large Scale Potential Field,' showing velocity flow lines converging toward massive attractors like the Shapley Supercluster and Lepus, illustrating the gravitational dynamics described. The visualization evolves into a 'Wiener Filter Density Field reconstruction,' highlighting galaxy clusters such as Hercules, Perseus-Pisces, and Coma with flow lines, further depicting the cosmic web's density and motion. The narrator emphasizes that the Shapley Supercluster drags the Great Attractor and all galaxies in our supercluster along with it. The scene culminates in a 3D representation titled 'The Laniakea Watershed,' a translucent blue structure with labeled regions and flow lines converging toward attractors, also highlighting the 'Virtual Repeller.' The narrator concludes by explaining that this crucial understanding allowed astronomers at Hawaii University to redefine the boundaries of "our local supercluster" or "galactic colony" by mapping the effects of the Great Attractor and the more remote Shapley Attractor.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed 2D velocity map of the Laniakea Supercluster, showing white flow lines converging toward massive attractors, as a male narrator explains the effort to define it. The scene transitions to a dark, star-filled sky, zooming into a dense cluster of stars before revealing an edge-on spiral galaxy, while the narrator hints at an undiscovered piece of the cosmic mystery. He elaborates that after the Shapley attractor was identified, astronomers speculated about another even more remote galaxy supercluster influencing our perceived movement across space. A portrait of Renée Kraan-Korteweg, a woman with short gray hair, then appears, followed by a split-screen showing her alongside a colorful starfield with galaxies. The narrator reveals that only five years ago, Renée Kraan-Korteweg and her team, who previously discovered the Norma cluster, finally solved this decades-long mystery. In 2016, her team announced the discovery of a new supercluster in the Zone of Avoidance, even further beyond the Shapley Supercluster, as a scientific paper titled 'Discovery of a supercluster in the Zone of Avoidance in Vela' is displayed. The video then shows images of the Anglo-Australian Telescope (AAT) and the Southern African Large Telescope (SALT) side-by-side, with the narrator explaining that data from these telescopes helped them identify a region of overdensity consistent with a galactic supercluster. A histogram of galaxy velocities, peaking around 18,000 km/s, visually confirms the existence of this new Vela Supercluster. Throughout, a calm, slightly mysterious background music plays.

The scene shifts to a constellation map, displaying constellations like Centaurus, Lupus, Crux, and Norma overlaid on the galactic plane with Earth visible, before panning across the dusty, dark band of the galactic plane. A spiral galaxy is then shown face-on and edge-on, emphasizing its structure. The narrator returns, asking reflectively, "So with all these things considered, what will be the outcome for us? Will we be pulled into the center?"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a 3D visualization of 'The Laniakea Watershed,' a translucent blue structure illustrating the large-scale flow of galaxies toward massive attractors like Shapley, Coma, and Perseus-Pisces, with a 'Virtual Repeller' highlighted. A male voice discusses the 'greater attractor' and 'the rest of the Laniakea Supercluster,' questioning if it will 'destroy our galaxy' in the process, then reassuringly states the answer is 'almost certainly no.' The scene transitions to a dramatic, edge-on spiral galaxy, its bright core and dark dust lanes visible, which then morphs into a flat, horizontal galactic plane with a grid overlay. The voice explains that while we are being 'lugged in the direction of these superclusters against the expansion of the universe,' the 'force that drives the Hubble flow is accelerating.' The camera zooms out to reveal a vast starfield with scattered galaxies, including a prominent spiral galaxy and a dense cluster of stars. The speaker asserts that 'we will never reach the Shapley Supercluster or even the Great Attractor,' as 'dark energy will have inflated the universe so aggressively' that the influences of the 'Great and Shapley attractors will no longer be able to overcome it.' The view shifts to a face-on spiral galaxy, followed by a thin, edge-on galaxy, then a dynamic interaction between two merging galaxies forming a single, luminous elliptical galaxy. The voice clarifies that although we are moving towards Centaurus, our only destination is 'a collision with Andromeda and anything else which remains in the local group.' The final frames show a dense, spherical cluster of stars fading into a dark, star-filled void, as the voice concludes that 'our only eventual destination is the void,' and 'The Great Attractor, like all galaxy clusters and groups, will be dispersed into its constituent galaxies.'

entity_library.Galaxy Filaments: Long, thread-like structures composed of interconnected galaxies, shown in high-contrast white against black space, forming the backbone of the cosmic web, visually representing the 'plethora of flows across space' that interlink the superclusters, as described by the narrator.
entity_library.Galaxy Clusters & Galaxy Superclusters: Dense groupings of galaxies shown as blue dots on a scientific map, plotted against galactic longitude and latitude, with labeled regions including the Shapley Supercluster and Great Attractor. The audio frequently mentions 'the greater attractor,' 'the Laniakea Supercluster,' 'these superclusters,' 'the Shapley Supercluster,' and 'the Great Attractor,' discussing their gravitational pull and eventual dispersal.
entity_library.Local Group Diagram: A schematic representation centered on the Local Group, with labeled arrows pointing to nearby galaxy groups and their distances in light-years, such as Virgo Cluster (65,000,000 ly) and M81 Group (11,000,000 ly). The narrator describes the Local Group as part of a larger 'colony' and uses it as a reference point for the Virgo Supercluster's mass.
entity_library.Virgo Cluster Visualization: A dense, glowing cluster of galaxies in warm hues, with a mix of spiral and elliptical galaxies, labeled 'Virgo Cluster' in white text. The narrator explicitly names it the "Virgo Cluster," stating it contains "more than 1,300 galaxies" and is the "gravitational core" to which our Local Group is an outlying region.
entity_library.Extended Galaxy Map: A large-scale 2D diagram showing the Local Group and surrounding galaxy groups within a 10 million light-year radius, with labeled clusters like Fornax and Eridanus, and a scale bar indicating 10 million light-years. This visual reinforces the narrator's discussion of mapping intergalactic space and understanding our large-scale surroundings.
entity_library.Laniakea Supercluster Visualization: A dynamic, golden-hued 3D representation of the Laniakea supercluster, depicted as a complex web of filaments with galaxies clustered along them, accompanied by the text 'Laniakea' and its etymological breakdown into 'Lani' (Heaven) and 'akea' (Immeasurable, Spacious). The narrator explains it extends over half a billion light-years, hosts over 100,000 galaxies, and consists of four main galaxy superclusters (Virgo, Hydra-Centaurus, Pavo-Indus, and Southern Supercluster), noting it's not dense enough to be gravitationally bound.
entity_library.Scientific Paper Display: A full-screen view of a research paper titled 'Discovery of a supercluster in the Zone of Avoidance in Vela,' co-authored by Renée C. Kraan-Korteweg, detailing the 2016 discovery of a new supercluster in the Zone of Avoidance, as announced by the male narrator.
entity_library.Laniakea Supercluster 2D Velocity Map: A colorful 2D map showing the Laniakea Supercluster outlined in orange, with labeled regions like Shapley, Coma, and Perseus-Pisces, and white flow lines converging toward massive attractors, with axes labeled in km/s, explicitly mentioned by the male narrator as being defined.
entity_library.Wiener Filter 3D Velocity Flow Lines: A 3D wireframe cube illustrating large-scale velocity flow lines, labeled 'Wiener Filter Large Scale velocity flow lines,' with axes marked in km/s, showing the motion of galaxies within the supercluster.
entity_library.Cylindrical Universe Map: A cylindrical 2D map of the universe densely populated with labeled galaxy groups and superclusters such as Virgo, Centaurus, and Hydra, with a yellow dashed line tracing the boundary of the Laniakea Supercluster. The narrator specifically names the Virgo and Hydra-Centaurus Superclusters as key components of Laniakea.
entity_library.The Laniakea Watershed: A 3D translucent blue structure showing the flow of galaxies toward massive attractors, with labeled regions like Shapley, Coma, and Perseus-Pisces, and the 'Virtual Repeller' highlighted in bold text. The audio refers to 'the rest of the Laniakea Supercluster' in the context of gravitational attraction and the potential destruction of our galaxy.
entity_library.The Great Attractor: A bright orange region in the 2D velocity map, marked by a black arrow, representing a massive gravitational attractor pulling galaxies toward it, located near the Centaurus Cluster. The narrator describes it as a key component of a 'gigantic flow mechanism' that pulls galaxies inward, contrasting it with the repeller.
entity_library.Galactic Plane: A dense, dusty band stretching horizontally across the 2D map, representing the plane of the Milky Way, which contributes to the 'zone of avoidance' where objects were 'undetectable through visible light'.
entity_library.ZONE OF AVOIDANCE: White, capitalized text overlaid on the galactic plane, indicating the region obscured by interstellar dust, preventing clear observation of distant galaxies, and explicitly referred to by the narrator as 'the zone of avoidance' that remained uncharted for many years, which the CIZA project aimed to map.
entity_library.CMB Dipole: A rainbow-colored, oval-shaped map showing a gradient from blue to red, representing the directional temperature variation in the Cosmic Microwave Background, with a red arrow indicating the direction of motion. The narrator names it as the 'CMB dipole', describing it as 'a gradient in the temperature of the sky from one side to the other', and explaining that this 'disparity is the result of the Milky Way moving through space', causing the 'red area' to appear 'slightly warmer' due to compressed radiation.
entity_library.Cosmic Microwave Background: A colorful, oval-shaped map displaying temperature fluctuations across the early universe, labeled 'COSMIC MICROWAVE BACKGROUND', which the narrator identifies as 'the primordial microwave radiation left over from the Big Bang which permeates all of space'.
entity_library.Constellation Map: A starfield with white outlines connecting stars to form constellations such as Centaurus, Lupus, Crux, and Norma, overlaid on the galactic plane, with the 'Norma cluster' specifically mentioned in the audio as contributing to gravitational influence.
entity_library.2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey: A scientific 2D map displaying the distribution of galaxies in a wedge-shaped slice of the universe, with axes labeled in redshift (0.05–0.20) and billion light-years (0.50–1.50), showing a detailed cosmic web structure. The male speaker refers to 'redshift surveys' as the method used to map the universe in 3D, leading to new discoveries about cosmic structure and gravitational anomalies.
entity_library.Distance Text Overlay: White text appearing on screen reading '150,000,000 – 220,000,000 ly,' indicating the distance to a prominent spiral galaxy in light-years, positioned to the right of the galaxy in the starfield. The male speaker's narration concludes by stating that the source of a gravitational anomaly is estimated to lie 'between 150 million and 220 million,' directly corresponding to this visual information.
entity_library.Prof. Donald Lynden-Bell: A black-and-white portrait of a bald man in a suit and tie, seated with an open book, with text below reading 'Prof. Donald Lynden-Bell 1935–2018'. The narrator identifies him as the leader of an international research team that proposed the concept of the Great Attractor, explaining that the term was coined based on their findings.
entity_library.Radio Waves: Red wavy lines in an animated diagram, traveling from Earth through the Milky Way toward 'HIDDEN GALAXIES', illustrating how radio signals 'penetrate the galaxy's clouds,' as explained by the narrator.
entity_library.Hidden Galaxies: Faint, distant galaxies labeled in the diagram and later revealed as glowing blue and red nebulae emerging from behind the galactic dust. The narrator refers to them as 'galaxy superclusters' that emit radio and X-ray signals, allowing them to be observed beyond the avoidance zone.
entity_library.Milky Way: Marked with a red cross on the blue cosmic map, positioned near the Great Attractor, indicating our galaxy's location within the larger cosmic structure, implicitly referred to by the narrator's questions about 'our peculiar velocity' and 'us' being 'pulled into the center'.
entity_library.Radio Telescope: A large, mesh-dish radio telescope with a complex metal framework, shown in black and white against a cloudy sky, symbolizing the tool used to detect cosmic radio waves, which the narrator highlights as a technology that has vastly improved, allowing observation beyond visible light.
entity_library.X-Rays: A green-tinted, grainy visualization of the galactic plane labeled 'X-RAYS', revealing structures hidden from optical view, followed by a white X-ray-like image showing the galactic plane's skeletal structure. The narrator explicitly defines them as 'another kind of electromagnetic radiation' and 'the best means we have of seeing behind the zone of avoidance,' capable of passing 'through the opaque galactic plane' and emitted by 'galaxy superclusters.'
entity_library.Galaxy Nebulae: Glowing blue and red nebulae appearing in the final frames, representing the revealed 'Hidden Galaxies' behind the galactic dust, shown as luminous, cloud-like structures in space.
entity_library.Röntgensatellit (ROSAT): A detailed satellite with solar panels and scientific instruments, shown in two views, identified as part of the 'ROSAT microwave mission' used to identify X-ray emitters and sources, crucial for revealing previously hidden galaxy clusters.
entity_library.Renée Kraan-Korteweg: A woman with short gray hair, wearing a light-colored shirt and a necklace, seated outdoors with green foliage behind her, identified by the male narrator as Renée Kraan-Korteweg, whose team, also responsible for discovering the Norma cluster, solved a decades-long cosmic mystery by discovering a new supercluster in 2016.
entity_library.Norma Cluster: A central cluster highlighted in green on a pixelated map, explicitly named 'Abell 3627' and 'the Norma Cluster,' which lies 'around 220 million light-years away from the Earth' and is thought to be 'very close to the proposed heart of the Great Attractor'.
entity_library.Abell 3627: A green-tinted, pixelated map showing a central cluster, explicitly named 'Abell 3627' and later identified as 'the Norma Cluster,' which was revealed by plotting X-ray sources from ROSAT.
entity_library.Cosmic Web Visualization: A dynamic 2D map of the universe showing the Milky Way at the center, with numerous labeled galaxy clusters and superclusters, forming a complex network of cosmic structures, is shown as the narrator mentions observing 'brilliant cosmic structures like Laniakea' and discusses the propagation of gravity.
entity_library.Mass Text Overlay: White text reading '1,000,000,000,000,000 Solar Masses' appearing on screen next to the large elliptical galaxy, which the narrator describes as a "region of one quadrillion times that of our sun."
entity_library.Dark Energy: White text appearing on a starfield with a grid overlay, indicating a cosmological force driving the accelerated expansion of the universe. The audio explains that 'dark energy will have inflated the universe so aggressively' that it will overcome the gravitational influences of attractors.
entity_library.DARK FLOW: White text overlaid on a colorful, animated Earth with wind patterns, explicitly named and defined by the narrator as a 'non-random component of the universe's expansion' that 'deviates from the Hubble flow,' causing galaxies to 'stream towards a single point like the great attractor,' and compared to 'enormous jet stream air currents.'
entity_library.Dipole Repeller: A large cosmic void shown in a 3D schematic diagram with blue flow lines radiating outward, labeled 'Dipole Repeller,' acting as a gravitational repeller in the large-scale structure of the universe, contrasting with the 'Great Attractor' which is discussed by the narrator in relation to the Shapley Attractor.
entity_library.Virtual Repeller: Bold white text appearing on screen, introducing a gravitational repeller concept in the cosmic structure, positioned to the right of the 3D Laniakea Watershed visualization, and explicitly named and described by a male narrator as a 'so-called virtual repeller'.
entity_library.Clusters in the Zone of Avoidance [CIZA]: White text on a black background, introducing the scientific concept of galaxy clusters hidden behind the Milky Way’s dust, serving as a title card for the segment, and explicitly named by a male narrator as 'the Clusters in the Zone of Avoidance project, or CIZA'.
entity_library.Shapley Supercluster: A detailed star chart labeled 'Shapley Supercluster,' displaying numerous galaxy clusters, also referred to in the audio as the 'Shapley Attractor,' whose influence helps explain the Great Attractor's effects.
entity_library.Great Attractor: A labeled region on the blue map, shown as a bright blue cluster with arrows pointing toward it, indicating its gravitational influence on surrounding galaxies, whose 'observed effects' are finally explained by the combined influence of other clusters.
entity_library.New Entity: A black-and-white portrait of a young man with neatly combed hair, wearing a suit and tie, looking directly at the camera, identified by the narrator as Harlow Shapley, a historical astronomer associated with the study of the Shapley Supercluster.
entity_library.Shapley Concentration (a.k.a. Shapley At: A colorful, heat-map visualization of the Shapley Concentration, labeled with specific galaxy clusters like SC1327-312 and A3558, showing the densest regions in red and yellow, with a scale bar indicating 1 Mpc, which the narrator explicitly identifies as the 'Shapley Attractor,' a concentrated region of galaxies discovered by CIZA, described as an even greater and more massive galactic overdensity than the Great Attractor, lying around half a billion light-years away.
entity_library.Wiener Filter Large Scale Potential Fiel: A 3D wireframe cube with velocity flow lines and labeled attractors like Shapley Supercluster and Lepus, illustrating large-scale gravitational dynamics. The narrator explains that everything in 'our supercluster' flows towards the 'Great Attractor,' which itself is pulled towards the 'Shapley Supercluster,' both of which are key attractors depicted here.
entity_library.Wiener Filter Density Field reconstructi: A 3D visualization showing galaxy clusters such as Hercules, Perseus-Pisces, and Coma as colored regions with flow lines converging toward them, representing density and motion in the cosmic web, illustrating the gravitational dynamics discussed by the narrator.
entity_library.Anglo-Australian Telescope [AAT]: A large white dome telescope with a surrounding fence and greenery, explicitly mentioned by the male narrator as a source of data used to identify the new supercluster.
entity_library.Southern African Large Telescope [SALT]: A large white dome telescope with a distinctive geometric dome and a tall chimney-like structure, explicitly mentioned by the male narrator as a source of data used to identify the new supercluster.
entity_library.The Vela supercluster: A histogram showing the number of galaxies (N) versus velocity (V_h [km sec⁻¹]), with a prominent peak around 18,000 km/s, visually confirming the existence of the Vela Supercluster, which the male narrator states was identified as a region of overdensity using telescope data.
entity_library.Vela Supercluster: A labeled region on the blue cosmic map, shown as a bright blue cluster to the left of the Shapley Supercluster, explicitly named in the audio as the 'Vela Supercluster,' described as 'around 800 million light years away,' 'the final piece of the puzzle,' and contributing 'at least 50 kilometers a second to our peculiar velocity,' playing a key role in explaining the Great Attractor's effects.
entity_library.V-web + flow lines for 2 local watershed: A 3D scientific visualization showing a translucent blue and red structure with flow lines representing the motion of galaxies toward massive attractors, labeled at the top of the frame, visually accompanies the narrator's description of 'galaxies flowing like great lakes across space' and the remarkable propagation of gravity.
entity_library.The End: White serif text appearing on a black background, signaling the conclusion of the video.
entity_library.Exoplanet System: A small, blue-tinted planet with visible surface markings transiting in front of a large, orange star, set against a starfield, representing a distant planetary system.
